summary: John Santerre, a PhD student and former photojournalist, shares lessons from a decade of working with nonprofits and social good organizations as a data scientist. He argues that the most important skill is educating organizations about your capabilities and limitations, while accepting that your most sophisticated technical skills may go unused. The work requires flexibility, solo resilience, and redefining success metrics—measuring impact by organizational change rather than technical achievement. He concludes that meaningful work doesn't require nonprofit status, and that being effective means embracing a generalist advocacy role rather than pushing specialist expertise.
key_points: Working at nonprofits means accepting a 'Faustian bargain'—your finely tuned skills will often fall on deaf ears, and your most useful contribution may be far simpler than your capability
You will likely work alone without peers to brainstorm or advocate with you, making it harder to build credibility and share the burden of navigating new environments
Success metrics differ radically: impact should be measured by how you change how an organization approaches problems, not by technical sophistication or prediction accuracy
Social good work exists across organizational types—a for-profit preventing drug addiction relapse may be more impactful than a nonprofit tracking bird migrations
Being effective requires shifting from specialist to generalist mindset, serving as an advocate rather than pushing the boundaries of your craft
money_quotes: The most rewarding and challenging aspect of working at a nonprofit is the responsibility you bear to educate the organization about the limitations and potential you present.
Just as the violinist Joshua Bell was ignored in the DC Metro but adored in the philharmonic, so too will many of your finely tuned skills fall on comparatively (computationally?) deaf ears.
In my experience, you have/get to 'check' your craft at the door.
The inculcation required to become a specialist in our craft can blind us to the impact our client requires.
I have to judge my contribution not by the number of trailing digits of prediction accuracy, but by the impact I have on how biologist's approach the problem.
Social good doesn't require a 501c3 status.
I find I am overwhelmed with options, but only if I am flexible in what role I expect to provide.
key_insight: Effective data science for social good requires checking your specialist ego at the door and measuring success by organizational impact rather than technical sophistication.
